steps.title: Identify what the question asks for
steps.content:
The question asks which equation displays its minimum value as a constant or coefficient—meaning the minimum value must appear directly as one of the stated numbers in the given form.

For example, vertex form $y = a(x - h)^2 + k$ displays the minimum $k$.

We need to check whether the minimum of function I or II equals any of its stated coefficients.

steps.title: Analyze function I on the domain $x \gt 0$
steps.content:
For $f(x) = 20(1.3)^{x+4}$:
  • The base is $1.3 \gt 1$, so this is an exponential growth function
  • As $x$ increases from just above 0, the exponent $x + 4$ increases, making $(1.3)^{x+4}$ larger
  • Therefore $f(x)$ is strictly increasing on $(0, \infty)$ with no actual minimum
  • The infimum (greatest lower bound) is $\lim_{x \to 0^+} 20(1.3)^{x+4} = 20(1.3)^4 \approx 57.1$
The displayed coefficients are 20, 1.3, and 4. The infimum 57.1 is not one of these.

steps.title: Analyze function II on the domain $x \gt 0$
steps.content:
For $g(x) = 20(1.12)(1.3)^{x+2}$:
  • The base is again $1.3 \gt 1$, so this is also exponential growth
  • For $x \gt 0$, the exponent $x + 2$ increases, so the function increases
  • This function is also strictly increasing on $(0, \infty)$ with no actual minimum
  • The infimum is $\lim_{x \to 0^+} 20(1.12)(1.3)^{x+2} = 20(1.12)(1.3)^2 \approx 37.9$
The displayed coefficients are 20, 1.12, 1.3, and 2. The infimum 37.9 is not one of these.

steps.title: Conclusion: neither equation displays its minimum as a coefficient
steps.content: Both functions are increasing exponentials whose infimums are never actually attained on the domain $x \gt 0$. Even if we consider the infimum, it is not a displayed constant or coefficient in either equation. That confirms D is correct.
